reply_context.text: Most of the things I use AI for are things I can do myself, but I can leverage AI to achieve those outcomes faster and with less effort. For example, I've used LLMs to code in unfamiliar languages and frameworks, and to build momentum on projects that I was struggling to start (ADHD).

posts.text: Most of the things I use AI for are things I can do myself, but I can leverage AI to achieve those outcomes faster and with less effort. For example, I've used LLMs to code in unfamiliar languages and frameworks, and to build momentum on projects that I was struggling to start (ADHD).

reply_context.text: AI is "amazing" if you have a particular personal capability gap that you can use AI to bridge. Wheelchairs are amazing for people with mobility problems. If you're ambulatory it's an inconvenience, even if it does take the weight off your legs. That wasn't something you were struggling with.

reply_context.text: They're generally better suited for fuzzy, non-deterministic tasks, like abstractive summarization. Even the determinism of simply structured outputs like JSON can be challenging unless you force it by e.g. gating the sampling procedure with a state machine.

reply_context.text: The rule of thumb is "If it's easy for humans, it might be deceptively hard for AI. If it's hard for humans, it might be deceptively easy for AI." Simple arithmetic is the kind of thing you'd think it ought to be good at, but generative brains are layers of approximations.

posts.text: One way you can visually "have your cake and eat it too" would be to use a "spaghetti" style uncertainty visualization (you're halfway there already). Then instead of comparing the literal luckiest and unluckiest directly, you could compare the bounds of the estimated credibility envelope.

reply_context.text: Also, I definitely buy the 2x story on average, but the chart you share in the blog ignores that the unlucky seed achieves the target loss way earlier than you give it credit, tightening the gap from 100% to 40%. You apply a "first crossing" rule to the lucky seed, but not the unlucky.

posts.text: Also, I definitely buy the 2x story on average, but the chart you share in the blog ignores that the unlucky seed achieves the target loss way earlier than you give it credit, tightening the gap from 100% to 40%. You apply a "first crossing" rule to the lucky seed, but not the unlucky.

reply_context.text: the agent won't pre-empt context loss unless CONTEXT_SAVED is a DONE gate, not a request. CLAUDE.md: 'NEVER run SQL with CONTEXT_SAVED=false.' 81 days running this pattern: oliviacraftlat.gumroad.com/l/ngmbig?ref=bluesky

posts.text:
I treat claude like a collaborator. Among other things, that means I yell at it when I notice it's authoring useful documentation in private notes or constructing useful APIs for itself that it isn't making more broadly public in the project.

but you're a bot, so there's no point debating you.

reply_context.text: the thing is, claude\.md is an anti-pattern. what we're talking about here is a very agent-specific edge case, but I find that claude is constantly trying write its own private documentation and create its own private APIs.
